revision.text:
[[神社]]において、親子関係にある[[神 (神道)|神]]が祀られる場合、子に当たる神のことを'''御子神'''（みこがみ）または'''苗裔神'''（びょうえいしん）という。
「御子神」という言い方をする場合は、親子ともに主神である場合や、親神が主神でその子も共に祀る場合である。特に、母子関係にある場合は、[[母子神信仰]]との関連もある。
例えば、[[八坂神社]]は主神として[[スサノオ|素戔嗚尊]]を中央に祀り、東に妃神の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛稲田姫命]]、西に「八柱御子神」を祀っている。[[春日大社]]には摂社の「若宮社」があり、主神の[[天児屋命|天児屋根命]]の御子神である天押雲根命を祀っている。
この「[[若宮]]」は、多くの場合主神の御子神を祀る神社であるが、祀られる神ではなく社が若いという意味で、後からできた社に「若宮」と名附ける場合もある。その場合は、主神の親神や祖先神が祀られていることや、主神の御旅所、主神を勧請した神社を若宮と呼んでいる例もある。よって、必ずしも若宮に御子神が祀られているとは限らない。
[[Category:日本の神|みこかみ]] [[de:Mikokami]]

revision.comment: ロボットによる 追加: [[ru:Микоками]]

[[ja:%E5%BE%A1%E5%AD%90%E7%A5%9E]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Mikogami]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Mikogami]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[de:Mikokami]] [[zh:%E5%BE%A1%E5%AD%90%E7%A5%9E]] {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} - Refers to child deities in Shinto shrines, worshipped alongside their parent deities. Also known as "{{ill|Byoei-shin|ja|苗裔神}}." The term is used when both parent and child deities are main deities or worshipped together. For example, at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊}} is enshrined as the main deity, with his consort {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫命}} to the east and their "{{ill|Eight Pillar Mikogami|ja|八柱御子神}}" to the west.

revision.text:
'''Mikogami''' (御子神) refers to child deities worshipped in Shinto shrines when there is a parent-child relationship among the enshrined deities. They are also known as '''Byoei-shin''' (苗裔神). <!--Mikogami--> <!--Mikogami-->
The term "Mikogami" is used when both the parent and child deities are considered main deities or when the child deity is worshipped alongside the parent deity. In cases of mother-child relationships, this is often related to the concept of {{ill|Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja|母子神信仰|12=draft}}.
For example, at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=draft}} is enshrined as the main deity, with his consort {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫命|12=draft}} to the east and their "{{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|12=draft}}" to the west. At {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha|12=draft}}, the auxiliary shrine "{{ill|Wakamiya Shrine|ja|若宮社|12=draft}}" enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=draft}}, the Mikogami of the main deity {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=draft}}.
The term "{{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮|12=draft}}" usually refers to shrines dedicated to Mikogami. However, in some cases, it refers to newer shrines rather than deities themselves. Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as {{ill|Otabisho|ja|御旅所|12=draft}}, or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ({{ill|Kanjō|ja|勧請|12=draft}}). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.
== Related Pages == * {{ill|Ōji-no-Kami|ja|王子神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|lt=Hachiōji Gongen|12=draft}}
== References == * {{Cite book|ja|author=Kokugakuin University Institute of Japanese Culture Research|year=1999|title=Shinto Encyclopedia|publisher=Kobundo}}
{{ill|Shinto|ja|神道 横|12=draft}} {{Shinto-stub}} ]]{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese Deities]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:500 shortest pages as of 03-29-2025]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[ja:%E5%BE%A1%E5%AD%90%E7%A5%9E]] [[sn:Mikogami]] [[en:Mikogami]] [[de:Mikokami]] [[zh:%E5%BE%A1%E5%AD%90%E7%A5%9E]] [[ja:御子神]] [[sn:ikogami]]

12 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:御子神 up to rev 98929103
revision.text:
'''御子神'''（みこがみ）は、[[神社]]において親子関係にある[[神 (神道)|神]]が祀られる場合、子に当たる神のこと。'''苗裔神'''（びょうえいしん）ともいう。
「御子神」という呼び方をするのは、親子ともに主神である場合や、親神が主神でその子も共に祀る場合である。特に、母子関係にある場合は、[[母子神信仰]]との関連もある。
例えば、[[八坂神社]]は主神として[[スサノオ|素戔嗚尊]]を中央に祀り、東に妃神の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛稲田姫命]]、西に「八柱御子神」を祀っている。[[春日大社]]には摂社の「若宮社」があり、主神の[[天児屋命|天児屋根命]]の御子神である[[天押雲根命]]を祀っている。
この「[[若宮]]」は、多くの場合主神の御子神を祀る神社であるが、祀られる神ではなく社が若いという意味で、後からできた社に「若宮」と名附ける場合もある。その場合は、主神の親神や祖先神が祀られていることや、主神の[[御旅所]]、主神を[[勧請]]した神社を若宮と呼んでいる例もある。よって、必ずしも若宮に御子神が祀られているとは限らない。
==関連項目== * [[王子神]] ==参考文献== * {{Cite book ja-jp |author = 國學院大學日本文化研究所 |year = 1999 |title = 神道事典 |publisher = 弘文堂 }}
{{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:みこかみ}} [[Category:日本の神]]

'''Mikogami''' (御子神) refers to child deities worshipped in Shinto shrines when there is a parent-child relationship among the enshrined deities. They are also known as '''Byoei-shin''' (苗裔神).
The term "Mikogami" is used when both the parent and child deities are considered main deities or when the child deity is worshipped alongside the parent deity. In cases of mother-child relationships, this is often related to the concept of {{ill|Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja|母子神信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mother-Child Deity Worship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
For example, at [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]], {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} is enshrined as the main deity, with his consort {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}} to the east and their "{{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}" to the west. At [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]], the auxiliary shrine "{{ill|Wakamiya Shrine|ja|若宮社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Shrine|qq=}}" enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, the Mikogami of the main deity {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天児屋命}}.
The term "[[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]]" usually refers to shrines dedicated to Mikogami. However, in some cases, it refers to newer shrines rather than deities themselves. Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as [[:en:Otabisho|Otabisho]], or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ([[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.

'''Mikogami''' (御子神) refers to child deities worshipped in Shinto shrines when there is a parent-child relationship among the enshrined deities. They are also known as '''Byoei-shin''' (苗裔神).
The term "Mikogami" is used when both the parent and child deities are considered main deities or when the child deity is worshipped alongside the parent deity. In cases of mother-child relationships, this is often related to the concept of {{ill|Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja|母子神信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
For example, at [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]], {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} is enshrined as the main deity, with his consort {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}} to the east and their "{{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}" to the west. At [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]], the auxiliary shrine "{{ill|Wakamiya Shrine|ja|若宮社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Shrine|qq=}}" enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, the Mikogami of the main deity {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天児屋命}}.
The term "[[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]]" usually refers to shrines dedicated to Mikogami. However, in some cases, it refers to newer shrines rather than deities themselves. Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as [[:en:Otabisho|Otabisho]], or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ([[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.
== Related Pages == * {{ill|Ōji-no-Kami|ja|王子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|lt=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Mikokami]][[en:Mikogami]][[ja:御子神]][[zh:御子神]]
'''Mikogami''' (御子神) refers to child deities worshipped in Shinto shrines when there is a parent-child relationship among the enshrined deities. They are also known as '''Byoei-shin''' (苗裔神).
The term "Mikogami" is used when both the parent and child deities are considered main deities or when the child deity is worshipped alongside the parent deity. In cases of mother-child relationships, this is often related to the concept of {{ill|Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja|母子神信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
For example, at [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]], {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} is enshrined as the main deity, with his consort {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}} to the east and their "{{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}" to the west. At [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]], the auxiliary shrine "{{ill|Wakamiya Shrine|ja|若宮社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Shrine|qq=}}" enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, the Mikogami of the main deity {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天児屋命}}.
The term "[[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]]" usually refers to shrines dedicated to Mikogami. However, in some cases, it refers to newer shrines rather than deities themselves. Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as [[:en:Otabisho|Otabisho]], or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ([[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.
== Related Pages == * {{ill|Ōji-no-Kami|ja|王子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|lt=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}
== References == * {{Cite book|ja|author=Kokugakuin University Institute of Japanese Culture Research|year=1999|title=Shinto Encyclopedia|publisher=Kobundo}}
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|御子神|version=98929103|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]]
}}
[[ja:御子神]]
[[de:Mikokami]] [[zh:%E5%BE%A1%E5%AD%90%E7%A5%9E]] [[ja:御子神]]

The term "Mikogami" is used when both the parent and child deities are considered main deities or when the child deity is worshipped alongside the parent deity. For example, at [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]], {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} is enshrined as the main deity, with his consort {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}} to the east and their "{{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as [[:en:Otabisho|Otabisho]], or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ([[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.

Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as [[:en:Otabisho|Otabisho]], or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ([[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.

The term "Mikogami" is used when both the parent and child deities are considered main deities or when the child deity is worshipped alongside the parent deity. In cases of mother-child relationships, this is often related to the concept of {{ill|Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja|母子神信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
For example, at [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]], {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} is enshrined as the main deity, with his consort {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}} to the east and their "{{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}" to the west. At [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]], the auxiliary shrine "{{ill|Wakamiya Shrine|ja|若宮社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Shrine|qq=}}" enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, the Mikogami of the main deity {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天児屋命}}.
The term "[[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]]" usually refers to shrines dedicated to Mikogami. However, in some cases, it refers to newer shrines rather than deities themselves. Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as [[:en:Otabisho|Otabisho]], or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ([[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]]). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.
== Related Pages == * {{ill|Ōji-no-Kami|ja|王子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|lt=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}

But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}" to the west. At {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha}}, the auxiliary shrine "{{ill|Wakamiya Shrine|ja|若宮社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Shrine|qq=}}" enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, the Mikogami of the main deity {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天児屋命}}.
The term "{{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|WD=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya}}" usually refers to shrines dedicated to Mikogami. However, in some cases, it refers to newer shrines rather than deities themselves. Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as {{ill|Otabisho|en|Otabisho|WD=Q11489117|lt=Otabisho}}, or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ({{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement}}). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Mikokami]][[en:Mikogami]][[ja:御子神]][[zh:御子神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Mikokami]][[en:Mikogami]][[ja:御子神]][[zh:御子神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Mikokami]][[en:Mikogami]][[ja:御子神]][[zh:御子神]]
'''Mikogami''' (御子神) refers to child deities worshipped in Shinto shrines when there is a parent-child relationship among the enshrined deities. They are also known as '''Byoei-shin''' (苗裔神).
The term "Mikogami" is used when both the parent and child deities are considered main deities or when the child deity is worshipped alongside the parent deity. In cases of mother-child relationships, this is often related to the concept of {{ill|Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja|母子神信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
For example, at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}}, {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} is enshrined as the main deity, with his consort {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}} to the east and their "{{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}" to the west. At {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}}, the auxiliary shrine "{{ill|Wakamiya Shrine|ja|若宮社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Shrine|qq=}}" enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, the Mikogami of the main deity {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天児屋命}}.
The term "{{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|WD=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}}" usually refers to shrines dedicated to Mikogami. However, in some cases, it refers to newer shrines rather than deities themselves. Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as {{ill|Otabisho|en|Otabisho|WD=Q11489117|lt=Otabisho|1=Otabisho}}, or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ({{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement|1=Bunrei}}). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.

But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|lt=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}

<!--Mikogami--><!--Mikogami--> [[en:Mikogami]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:御子神]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:御子神]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:御子神]] {{wikidata link|Q352176}} [[Category:pages with a usurping enwiki page]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Mikokami]][[en:Mikogami]][[ja:御子神]][[zh:御子神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Mikokami]][[en:Mikogami]][[ja:御子神]][[zh:御子神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Mikokami]][[en:Mikogami]][[ja:御子神]][[zh:御子神]] {{Short description|Child deity in Shinto}} {{About|the type of Shinto deity|other uses|Mikogami (disambiguation)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=September 2025}} In [[Shinto]], a '''mikogami''' ({{Nihongo|御子神|honorable-child-kami}}) is a term used within the context of parent-child deity cults to refer to an offspring [[kami]].<ref name="Kokugakuin">"[https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9955 Mikogami]". ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 12 September 2025.</ref> A mikogami is also referred to as a ''byōeishin''. The parent deity of a mikogami can be either a father ('''hikogami''') or a mother deity (''[[himegami]]''), with the latter fitting into the framework of cults devoted to "mother-child deities"('''boshijin'''').<ref name="Kokugakuin"/>
The concept of a mikogami is illustrated in the fragmentary ''[[Tsukushi-no kuni]] [[fudoki]]'', which describes a three-peaked mountain called Kishimayama. The southwestern peak is named hikogami, the middle peak is the himegami, and the northeastern peak is the ''mikogami'' (offspring-kami).<ref name="Kokugakuin"/>
Mikogami is a "subordinate" deity ({{Nihongo|眷族|kenzoku}}).<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_k.html |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: K |website=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University |access-date=12 September 2025}}</ref>
==Wakamiya== A mikogami often is enshrined in an affiliated shrine, known as '''wakamiya'''.<ref name="Kokugakuin2">"[https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9945 Wakamiya]". ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 12 September 2025.</ref> While most of these are dedicated to the offspring of the central deity (''waka'' means "young"), some may enshrine a parent or ancestral deity. Furthermore, some ''wakamiya'' serve as temporary sites (''anzaisho'' or ''{{ill|karimiya|jp|行宮}}'') for the main deity during festivals. Therefore, the shrine being a wakamiya does not necessarily imply the enshrinement of a mikogami.<ref name="Kokugakuin"/>
==Boshijin== Term ''boshijin'' is used in case a joint shrine is dedicated to mother goddess (''boshin'') and her mikogami.<ref name="Kokugakuin3">"[https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9452 Boshijin]". ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 13 September 2025.</ref>
==Examples of enshrinement== [[File:Kasuga-taisha, Wakamiya-jinja, honden-2.jpg|thumb|Wakamiya Jinja at Kasuga-taisha]] Specific instances of mikogami enshrinement can be found at several prominent shrines in Japan:<ref name="Kokugakuin"/>
* [[Yasaka Shrine]]: The main hall is dedicated to [[Susanoo]] as the chief kami, with his consort [[Kushinadahime]] on the east. To the west, eight offspring deities, known as the ''yahashira no mikogami'' (eight pillar mikogami), are enshrined. These include [[Yashimajinumi]] no kami, {{ill|Itakeru no kami|ja|五十猛神}}, {{ill|Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime no kami|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ}}, [[Ōtoshi-no-kami|Ōtoshi no kami]], [[Ukanomitama]] no kami, Ōyatsuhiko no kami, and {{ill|Suseribime no mikoto|ja|スセリビメ}}. * [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]]: Within the shrine precincts, there is an associate shrine (''[[sessha]]'') dedicated to Sugawara Takachika and Sugawara Kageyuki, who are the offspring of the principal deity of Dazaifu Tenmangū, [[Sugawara no Michizane]]. * [[Kasuga-taisha]]: This is the clan shrine (''[[ujigami]]'') of the [[Fujiwara clan]], and one of the deities worshipped is [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestral kami (''[[Sojin (Shinto)|sojin]]'') of the [[Nakatomi clan]]. An affiliated shrine, ''Wakamiya Jinja'', enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命}}, the divine offspring of Ame-no-Koyane. The establishment of the wakamiya shrine at Kasuga in 1135 was also closely related to the belief in ''[[goryō]]'' (vengeful spirits of the high-ranking dead), which was popular in the late [[Heian period]].
''Boshijin'' can be found in [[Kamo shrines]] and [[Hachiman]] beliefs. In the latter case, Hachiman shrines enshrine both [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his mother, [[Empress Jingū]].<ref name="Kokugakuin3"/>
== See also == * [[Miko]], a Shinto shrine maiden
==References== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Shinto terminology]]

The term "{{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|WD=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}}" usually refers to shrines dedicated to Mikogami. However, in some cases, it refers to newer shrines rather than deities themselves. Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as {{ill|Otabisho|en|Otabisho|WD=Q11489117|lt=Otabisho|1=Otabisho}}, or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ({{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement|1=Bunrei}}). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.
== Related Pages == * {{ill|Ōji-no-Kami|ja|王子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|lt=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Mikokami]][[en:Mikogami]][[ja:御子神]][[zh:御子神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Mikokami]][[en:Mikogami]][[ja:御子神]][[zh:御子神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Mikokami]][[en:Mikogami]][[ja:御子神]][[zh:御子神]] {{Short description|Child deity in Shinto}} {{About|the type of Shinto deity|other uses|Mikogami (disambiguation)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=September 2025}} In [[Shinto]], a '''mikogami''' ({{Nihongo|御子神|honorable-child-kami}}) is a term used within the context of parent-child deity cults to refer to an offspring [[kami]].<ref name="Kokugakuin">"[https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9955 Mikogami]". ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 12 September 2025.</ref> A mikogami is also referred to as a ''byōeishin''. The parent deity of a mikogami can be either a father ('''hikogami''') or a mother deity (''[[himegami]]''), with the latter fitting into the framework of cults devoted to "mother-child deities"('''boshijin'''').<ref name="Kokugakuin"/>
The concept of a mikogami is illustrated in the fragmentary ''[[Tsukushi-no kuni]] [[fudoki]]'', which describes a three-peaked mountain called Kishimayama. The southwestern peak is named hikogami, the middle peak is the himegami, and the northeastern peak is the ''mikogami'' (offspring-kami).<ref name="Kokugakuin"/>
Mikogami is a "subordinate" deity ({{Nihongo|眷族|kenzoku}}).<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_k.html |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: K |website=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University |access-date=12 September 2025}}</ref>
==Wakamiya== A mikogami often is enshrined in an affiliated shrine, known as '''wakamiya'''.<ref name="Kokugakuin2">"[https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9945 Wakamiya]". ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 12 September 2025.</ref> While most of these are dedicated to the offspring of the central deity (''waka'' means "young"), some may enshrine a parent or ancestral deity. Furthermore, some ''wakamiya'' serve as temporary sites (''anzaisho'' or ''{{ill|karimiya|jp|行宮}}'') for the main deity during festivals. Therefore, the shrine being a wakamiya does not necessarily imply the enshrinement of a mikogami.<ref name="Kokugakuin"/>
==Boshijin== Term ''boshijin'' is used in case a joint shrine is dedicated to mother goddess (''boshin'') and her mikogami.<ref name="Kokugakuin3">"[https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9452 Boshijin]". ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 13 September 2025.</ref>
==Examples of enshrinement== [[File:Kasuga-taisha, Wakamiya-jinja, honden-2.jpg|thumb|Wakamiya Jinja at Kasuga-taisha]] Specific instances of mikogami enshrinement can be found at several prominent shrines in Japan:<ref name="Kokugakuin"/>
* [[Yasaka Shrine]]: The main hall is dedicated to [[Susanoo]] as the chief kami, with his consort [[Kushinadahime]] on the east. To the west, eight offspring deities, known as the ''yahashira no mikogami'' (eight pillar mikogami), are enshrined. These include [[Yashimajinumi]] no kami, {{ill|Itakeru no kami|ja|五十猛神}}, {{ill|Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime no kami|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ}}, [[Ōtoshi-no-kami|Ōtoshi no kami]], [[Ukanomitama]] no kami, Ōyatsuhiko no kami, and {{ill|Suseribime no mikoto|ja|スセリビメ}}. * [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]]: Within the shrine precincts, there is an associate shrine (''[[sessha]]'') dedicated to Sugawara Takachika and Sugawara Kageyuki, who are the offspring of the principal deity of Dazaifu Tenmangū, [[Sugawara no Michizane]]. * [[Kasuga-taisha]]: This is the clan shrine (''[[ujigami]]'') of the [[Fujiwara clan]], and one of the deities worshipped is [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestral kami (''[[Sojin (Shinto)|sojin]]'') of the [[Nakatomi clan]]. An affiliated shrine, ''Wakamiya Jinja'', enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命}}, the divine offspring of Ame-no-Koyane. The establishment of the wakamiya shrine at Kasuga in 1135 was also closely related to the belief in ''[[goryō]]'' (vengeful spirits of the high-ranking dead), which was popular in the late [[Heian period]].
''Boshijin'' can be found in [[Kamo shrines]] and [[Hachiman]] beliefs. In the latter case, Hachiman shrines enshrine both [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his mother, [[Empress Jingū]].<ref name="Kokugakuin3"/>
== See also == * [[Miko]], a Shinto shrine maiden
==References== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Shinto terminology]]
==Old== '''Mikogami''' (御子神) refers to child deities worshipped in Shinto shrines when there is a parent-child relationship among the enshrined deities. They are also known as '''Byoei-shin''' (苗裔神).
The term "Mikogami" is used when both the parent and child deities are considered main deities or when the child deity is worshipped alongside the parent deity. In cases of mother-child relationships, this is often related to the concept of {{ill|Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja|母子神信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
For example, at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}}, {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} is enshrined as the main deity, with his consort {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}} to the east and their "{{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}" to the west. At {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}}, the auxiliary shrine "{{ill|Wakamiya Shrine|ja|若宮社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Shrine|qq=}}" enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, the Mikogami of the main deity {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天児屋命}}.
The term "{{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|WD=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}}" usually refers to shrines dedicated to Mikogami. However, in some cases, it refers to newer shrines rather than deities themselves. Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as {{ill|Otabisho|en|Otabisho|WD=Q11489117|lt=Otabisho|1=Otabisho}}, or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ({{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement|1=Bunrei}}). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.
== Related Pages == * {{ill|Ōji-no-Kami|ja|王子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|lt=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}

''Encyclopedia of Shinto''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 12 September 2025.</ref> A mikogami is also referred to as a ''byōeishin''. ==Old== '''Mikogami''' (御子神) refers to child deities worshipped in Shinto shrines when there is a parent-child relationship among the enshrined deities. They are also known as '''Byoei-shin''' (苗裔神).
The term "Mikogami" is used when both the parent and child deities are considered main deities or when the child deity is worshipped alongside the parent deity. In cases of mother-child relationships, this is often related to the concept of {{ill|Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja|母子神信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mother-Child Deity Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
For example, at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}}, {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} is enshrined as the main deity, with his consort {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}} to the east and their "{{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|Note=I’m not sure the proper translation. But it is not Eight pillar mikogami, that is an error based on the counter for gods|Lt=|lt=Eight mikogami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}" to the west. At {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|qid=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}}, the auxiliary shrine "{{ill|Wakamiya Shrine|ja|若宮社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Shrine|qq=}}" enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}, the Mikogami of the main deity {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天児屋命}}.
The term "{{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|qid=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}}" usually refers to shrines dedicated to Mikogami. However, in some cases, it refers to newer shrines rather than deities themselves. Such shrines may enshrine the parent or ancestor deity of the main shrine, serve as {{ill|Otabisho|en|Otabisho|qid=Q11489117|lt=Otabisho|1=Otabisho}}, or be shrines established as a result of deity transfer ({{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|qid=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement|1=Bunrei}}). Therefore, not all Wakamiya shrines are dedicated to Mikogami.
